coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanosheets with dominant (001) facets for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Sui, Yulei
Wu, Ling
Zhong, Shengkui
Liu, Qingxia
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanosheets with a majority of (001) facet (CQDs/TiO2-001) samples are successfully prepared via a facile method. Compared to TiO2-001 and CQDs/P25, the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 presents a remarkably higher photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution with a considerable stability. XRD, XPS, HRTEM, FESEM, FTIR, Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–visible reflectance spectroscopy are adopted to investigate the morphology, structure and properties of synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001. The mechanism of the improved photocatalytic activity over CQDs/TiO2-001 is also investigated. The results show that the improved photocatalytic activity over CQDs/TiO2-001 can be attributed to the synergistic effects of TiO2-001 and CQDs: the highly exposed (001) facets of TiO2-001 promote the transportation of photogenerated electrons and the loading of CQDs restrains the recombination of electrons-holes on (001) facets. Meanwhile, the visible-light absorption is extended because the CQDs serve as a photosensitizer and sensitize TiO2-001 through the newly formed TiOC bond between the CQDs and TiO2-001.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
(001) facet
Photocatalytic H2 evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD analysis of the synthesized TiO2-001 and CQDs/TiO2-001.
(a) TEM image of the synthesized TiO2-001; (b) HRTEM image and the corresponding FFT pattern of the synthesized TiO2-001; (c) FESEM image of the synthesized TiO2-001.
TEM images (a)–(c) and HRTEM image (d) of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001.
XPS analysis of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TiO2-001 and CQDs/TiO2-001; (b) the fluorescence intensity by irradiating the CQDs/TiO2-001 under UV–Vis and Vis light.

(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution with commercial anatase and CQDs/anatase, commercial P25 and CQDs/P25, synthesized TiO2-001 and CQDs/TiO2-001 as catalysts under simulated solar light; (b) cycling test of CQDs/TiO2-001.
Illustration of the charge transfer and H2 evolution in TiO2-001 and synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanosheets with dominant (001) facets for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Iron and Steel, Soochow University, Suzhou 215131, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 1H9, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanosheets with a majority of (001) facet (CQDs/TiO2-001) samples are successfully prepared via a facile method. Compared to TiO2-001 and CQDs/P25, the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 presents a remarkably higher photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution with a considerable stability. XRD, XPS, HRTEM, FESEM, FTIR, Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–visible reflectance spectroscopy are adopted to investigate the morphology, structure and properties of synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001. The mechanism of the improved photocatalytic activity over CQDs/TiO2-001 is also investigated. The results show that the improved photocatalytic activity over CQDs/TiO2-001 can be attributed to the synergistic effects of TiO2-001 and CQDs: the highly exposed (001) facets of TiO2-001 promote the transportation of photogenerated electrons and the loading of CQDs restrains the recombination of electrons-holes on (001) facets. Meanwhile, the visible-light absorption is extended because the CQDs serve as a photosensitizer and sensitize TiO2-001 through the newly formed TiOC bond between the CQDs and TiO2-001.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the phenomenon of the photocatalytic H2 evolution by water splitting on a TiO2 electrode was discovered [1], effectively transforming solar energy into clean hydrogen fuel by environmental-friendly photocatalytic water splitting is an ultimate goal for scientists in the world [2–7]. Although many other semiconductors or composites have been investigated and reported in recent years [8–16], TiO2 is still the most widely used photocatalyst due to its excellent photocatalytic reactivity and chemical stability [17–29]. Loading noble metals (such as Pt, Au, et al.) [30–33] on the facets of TiO2 crystals is an effective strategy to improve its photocatalytic activity because it forms a Schottky junction at the noble metal/TiO2 interface, which benefits the enhancement of separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons-holes and the suppression of the electron-hole pairs recombination [20]. But the scarcity and valuableness of noble metal limit the development and the application of noble-metal/TiO2 in photocatalytic water splitting to a great extent. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted extensive attention due to its superior stability against photobleaching and size dependent photoluminescence [34–39]. Tang et al. [40] reported that the CQDs-TiO2 composites showed higher photocatalytic activity than pure TiO2. Yu et al. [34] pointed out that CQDs act as an electron reservoir to improve the efficient separation of the photoinduced electron–hole pairs under UV light. Although CQDs have been studied for photocatalytic applications, the interaction between CQDs and TiO2 nanosheets with the dominant high active (001) facet and its effect on the photocatalytic activity have not been studied yet.

Herein, we successfully developed a facile approach to load CQDs on TiO2 nanosheets with highly exposed (001) facets. The synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 exhibits a superior catalytic activity and stability in H2 evolution under simulated solar energy. Mechanisms of improved photocatalytic activity for CQDs/TiO2-001 are also proposed.

TiO2-001 nanoparticles were synthesized as follows [33], 5 mL of HF solution (with a concentration ca. 40 wt%) was dropwise added into a beaker which contains 30 mL Ti(OC4H9)4 with stirring for 20 min. Then the mixture was moved into a dried Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment (180 °C, 24 h). The precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed until no fluorine were detected in the supernatant of the solution. TiO2-001 nanosheets were obtained after freeze-drying the samples for several days.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized based on the reported electrochemical method [41]. Firstly, two graphite rods were inserted into a cell filled with distilled water serving as electrodes, and 40 V direct current voltages was applied between two electrodes. After electrochemical treatment for 5 days, a dark solution was formed. CQDs powder was collected after centrifugation and drying. Then, 20 mg as-prepared CQDs powder was added into 10 mL distilled water under stirring, and the standard CQDs solution (2 mg/mL) was obtained.

CQDs/TiO2-001 samples were synthesized as follows. 0.4 g of TiO2 nanosheets was added into 20 mL deionized water under vigorous string, then 4 mL CQDs (2 mg/mL, dispersed in deionized water) were dropwise added into the solution. A homogeneous suspension was obtained after a long time stirring. The suspension was sealed and heated at 120 °C for 4 h. And the resulting CQDs/TiO2-001 powder was obtained after centrifugation, washing and freeze-drying treatment. CQDs/anatase and CQDs/P25 are also synthesized using the similar method.

XRD patterns were obtained on a Rigaku XRD Ultimate IV diffractometer. Raman spectra were obtained on a Raman micro-spectroscopy with the range of Raman spectroscopic analysis 200–1800 cm−1. TEM and HRTEM images were achieved using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM 2100) at 200 kV. In order to investigate the morphology of as-prepared samples, Zeiss Sigma field emission scanning electron microscope was employed. XPS patterns were performed on the Kratos AXIS Ultra XPS Imaging Spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic source. The optical absorption spectra of the synthesized samples were recorded using a UV–Vis reflectance spectroscopy, and BaSO4 powder was used as a reflectance standard. Photoluminescence study was carried out at room temperature on a Fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). OH-trapping experiment was carried out on Fluorescence spectroscopy measurements. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer GX spectrophotometer.

Using a Xe lamp (300 W) as light source, photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reactions were conducted in a vessel reactor. It should be noted that the lamp is 15 cm far away to the vessel reactor. The rate of H2 evolution was determined in an aqueous methanol solution (60 mL, 20% v/v) containing 20 mg of photocatalysts. In order to completely remove the air and to assure anaerobic conditions in the system, argon gas (20 mL/min) was purged into the vessel reactor for 30 min before irradiation. During hydrogen evolution experiment, the evolved H2 gas was analyzed using a Hewlett-Packard model Agilent 6890N gas chromatograph (GC) equipped with a packed bed column (HaySep D). In cycling tests, the used photocatalyst in the last test was collected and dried carefully for the next photocatalytic activity test.
The XRD spectra for the as-prepared TiO2-001 and CQDs/TiO2-001 are shown in Fig. 1 .

As can be seen from Fig. 1, the well-resolved diffraction peaks of synthesized TiO2-001 at 25.3°, 37.9°, 48.2°, 53.9°, 55.2°, 62.6°, 70.3°, and 75.1° indicate that the synthesized TiO2-001 is indexed to the anatase structures (JCPDS card 21-1272) [42]. Obviously, the (004) diffraction peak of the synthesized TiO2-001 is more broadened than the standard pattern, suggesting the synthesized TiO2-001 has a very small size along the [001] direction. Meanwhile, the sharp (200) diffraction peak of the synthesized TiO2-001 demonstrates that the crystal size along the [100]/[010] directions is quite large. The results suggest that the synthesized TiO2-001 may have a highly exposed (001) facets. However, no any XRD diffraction peak related to CQDs was observed from the XRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2, indicating the low amount and the well dispersion of CQDs in the composites.

To further demonstrate the facets structure and the morphology of the synthesized TiO2-001, HRTEM and FESEM are performed on the TiO2-001 samples. As we can see from Fig. 2(a), the synthesized TiO2-001 performs a well-defined nanosheet structure with an average size of ~50 nm. The fringe spacing of 0.235 nm and the corresponding FFT pattern agrees well with the spacing of the (001) planes of anatase TiO2 (see Fig. 2(b)), suggesting the (001) facets were highly exposed in the synthesized TiO2-001. Fig. 2(c) shows the typical FESEM image of the synthesized TiO2-001. Obviously, the synthesized TiO2-001 has nanosheet structure with the thickness of about 4–6 nm.

The structure and shape of synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 are investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscope and the TEM/HRTEM images are shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen from Fig. 3(a), (b) and (c), a large number of nanodots with the size range of 2–5 nm are uniformly decorated on the surface of TiO2-001 nanosheets. The fringe spacing of 0.235 nm agrees well with the spacing of the (001) planes of TiO2-001, while the fringe spacing of 0.283 nm assigned to the graphitized carbon [43], indicating CQDs uniformly dispersed on the surface of TiO2-001 nanosheets and the great majority of CQDs are dispersed on (001) facets.

To further illustrate the interaction between CQDs and synthesized TiO2-001, XPS is performed on the CQDs/TiO2-001 (Fig. 4 ) and TiO2-001 (Fig. S1), respectively. As we can see from Fig. 4a, Ti, O, F and C are present in the full-scale XPS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2-001. Peaks at 458.5 and 464.4 eV in the XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b) are assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 binding energy respectively, indicating that the oxidation state of the Ti element in CQDs/TiO2-001 is the same as that of TiO2-001 (Fig. S1b). For the C 1s peaks of CQDs/TiO2-001, as can be seen from Fig. 4c, the typical peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.9 eV are attributed to the CC, CO and OCO bonds in CQDs/TiO2-001, respectively. However, no CO and OCO bonds are recorded in pure TiO2-001 (Fig. S1c), suggesting the presence of the CQDs and the connection between CQDs and TiO2-001 in the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 [34]. The O1 peaks are consisted of three different binding energy peaks at 529.8 eV, 531.5 eV and 532.5 eV (Fig. 4d), which can be attributed to TiO, OH and CO bonding respectively, further demonstrating the presence of the CQDs [44].

The Raman peaks (Fig. S2) of CQDs/TiO2-001 located at 396, 513 and 638 cm−1 are in accordance with the vibration of B1g, A1g and E1g of TiO2, indicating the chemical environment of Ti atoms is maintained and the only possible bond between CQDs and TiO2-001 is TiOC. FTIR spectra of the synthesized TiO2-001 and CQDs/TiO2-001 (Fig. S3) also confirm the presence of the skeletal vibration of CH, CO and COH in the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001. Hydroxyl (C–OH) and carboxyl (O=C–O) of CQDs could react with the hydroxyl (TiO2–OH) which located at the surface of TiO2 and formed TiOC bonds. Meanwhile, there is a shift of the absorption band below 1000 cm−1 between CQDs/TiO2-001 and TiO2-001, which is also confirmed the presence of TiOC vibrations.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized TiO2-001 and CQDs/TiO2-001 samples and the fluorescence intensity by irradiating the CQDs/TiO2-001 under the UV–Vis and Vis light are illustrated in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively.

As can be seen in Fig. 5(a), TiO2-001 has almost no absorption above 385 nm and that is why only UV light (~5% of total solar irradiation) can be absorbed by TiO2-001. Nevertheless, the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 shows an enhanced absorption in the range of 280–700 nm, indicating the extended visible-light absorption over the CQDs/TiO2-001. However, CQDs can hardly enhance the visible light responsive property [45], so the enhanced light absorption cannot be contributed to the generation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs on the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001.

In order to investigate the mechanism of extended visible-light absorption over the CQDs/TiO2-001, the quantity of OH in the photocatalytic reaction system was detected. According to the reported literature [34], the quantity of OH can be evaluated by monitoring the intensity of fluorescence which emitted by terephthalic acid to generate 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid at around 426 nm. The fluorescence upon the UV–Vis light irradiation and visible light irradiation of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 are shown in Fig. 5(b). As can be seen in Fig. 5(b), a significant fluorescence is observed upon the UV–Vis light irradiation while nearly no fluorescence is detected for the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 upon the visible light irradiation, indicating that a large amount of OH is generated upon the UV–Vis light irradiation and no OH is formed upon the visible light irradiation. Therefore, it can be concluded that there are no “electron–hole” pairs are formed from TiO2-001 under visible light, and the extended visible-light absorption behavior of CQDs/TiO2-001 should be attributed to the photosensitization effect of CQDs. The CQDs serve as a photosensitizer, sensitize TiO2-001 through the newly formed TiOC bond between the CQDs and TiO2-001, leading to the extended visible-light absorption over the CQDs/TiO2-001.

The photocatalytic activity of synthesized samples is evaluated by photocatalytic H2 production from methanol aqueous solution under a 300W Xenon lamp irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6(a), without CQDs loading, both TiO2-001, commercial anatase and P25 exhibit relatively low photocatalytic H2 evolution rates (<2 μmol/h), probably due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes. It is noted that the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of TiO2-001 is higher than commercial anatase but lower than P25. The highly exposed (001) facet of the synthesized TiO2-001 nanosheets promoted the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and that is why TiO2-001 shows a higher photocatalytic performance than commercial anatase. However, the dominant exposed (001) facet also leads to the high recombination rate of electrons and holes so that the photocatalytic activity of TiO2-001 is also restrained [46,47].

The photocatalytic H2 evolution rate is increased when CQDs are loaded on TiO2 samples, comparing with TiO2-001, commercial anatase and P25. The highest evolution rate (7.9 μmol/h), 4 times higher than that of TiO2-001, was observed for the CQDs/TiO2-001. This suggests that CQDs play an important role in the improvement of composites' photocatalytic activity. For example, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rates of TiO2-001 and P25 are 2.1 μmol/h and 1.8 μmol/h, respectively, while the H2 evolution rates are remarkable increased to 5.2 μmol/h and 7.9 μmol/h if 2 wt% CQDs are loaded on the surface of TiO2-001 and P25. More importantly, the CQDs/TiO2-001 composite shows a higher photocatalytic H2 evolution rate than CQDs/P25 composite. These results reveal that the effects of CQDs on the improvement of photocatalytic activity are different between TiO2-001 and P25. It must be noted that F− is still bound to the surface of TiO2-001 (Fig. 4). In order to determine the influence of surface fluorine on photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, we removed surface fluorine by calcining the catalysts at 550 °C for 2 h before testing for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution test results (Fig. S4) reveal that the surface fluorine play few influence on the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001.

The cycling test results of the photocatalytic activity of synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 for H2 evolution are shown in Fig. 6(b). The H2 evolution rate is maintained even after 8 cycles, indicating a great stability of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 in the photocatalytic H2 evolution under the simulated solar light irradiation.

Reaction mechanisms for the improved H2 evolution activity of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 are proposed and illustrated in Fig. 7 . For TiO2-001 and P25, the excited electrons from the valence band were generated by the light irradiation. Then the excited electrons migrate to the surface of TiO2 and then react with H+ ions adsorbed to form H2 [48,49]. Hydroxyl radical is generated by the accumulation of holes at the surface of TiO2, and then transformed into oxidation products through complicated reactions [33,50,51]. The high active (001) facet, on the one hand, promotes the transfer of photogenerated electrons. But on the other hand, it provides favorable condition for the recombination of electrons and holes [24,33,44]. The PL spectra (Fig. S5) show that the CQDs/TiO2-001 have the lower band–band PL intensity than TiO2-001, indicating loading CQDs on the surface of TiO2-001 decreased recombination rate of electrons and holes effectively. Meanwhile, CQDs loaded on the surface of TiO2-001 play a key role of electron reservoir to trap photogenerated electrons from the conduction band of TiO2-001, which not only facilitates the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs, but also inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [47]. In addition, the visible-light absorption is extended because the CQDs serve as a photosensitizer and sensitizes TiO2-001 through the newly formed TiOC bond between the CQDs and TiO2-001. Therefore, CQDs/TiO2-001 has a superior photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution.

In summary, CQDs/TiO2 nanosheets with highly exposed (001) facets are successfully synthesized via a facile method. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2-001 is lower than P25 while the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-001 exhibits a superior photocatalytic H2 evolution activity than CQDs/P25. The high active (001) facet of TiO2-001 promotes electrons transfer but electrons and holes are recombined very easily, which limits the photocatalytic activity. CQDs loading on the surface of TiO2-001 play a key role of electron reservoir to trap photogenerated electrons, which not only facilitates the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs, but also inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Meanwhile, the visible-light absorption is extended because the CQDs serve as a photosensitizer and sensitizes TiO2-001 through the newly formed TiOC bond between the CQDs and TiO2-001, consequently leads to superior photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2-001.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Canadian Centre for Clean Coal/Carbon and Mineral Processing Technologies (C5MPT), China Scholarship Council (CSC) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (2018M642310) for the financial supports, and the NanoFAB at the University of Alberta for providing access to make use of their instruments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.03.028.
